true_relatives.note: Direct source — सोम (Soma/Moon) + वासर (day) contracted to वार in modern Hindi
Both Monday and सोमवार mean 'Moon's day' — English from Old English mōnandæg, Hindi from Sanskrit soma (the Moon deity)
No shared meaning, but knowing Monday = Moon-day in both English and Hindi reinforces the pattern

useful_coincidences.meaning: body (medical term); also the sacred Vedic ritual drink
useful_coincidences.note: The word 'soma' appears unchanged in English scientific vocabulary, making सोम immediately recognisable as the Sanskrit original

sound_alikes.note: 'soma' (the Vedic ritual drink, also Greek/English for 'body') shares the exact opening syllable — सोम is the Moon god associated with that drink

root.meaning: the Moon; the Moon deity; the sacred Vedic ritual drink

root_breakdown.logic: सोम is the Sanskrit name for the Moon and the Moon deity; वार means 'day of the week'. So सोमवार is literally 'the Moon's day', exactly parallel to English Monday.

etymology_story: In ancient Sanskrit, soma referred both to the Moon and to the sacred ritual drink offered to the gods — both associated with the lunar cycle.
The word सोमवासर (Moon-day) was used in Sanskrit astronomical texts to name the first day of the week.
As Sanskrit evolved through Prakrit into Hindi, वासर shortened to वार, giving सोमवार — still transparently meaning 'the Moon's day'.

pronunciation_guide.stress: roughly even, with a slight lengthening on the final ā

tips.tip: the first syllable 'som' is exactly the word 'soma' minus the final 'a'
'vār' sounds like the Russian word вар (boiling, pitch) — same vowel and ending

memory_hook.primary: A glowing crescent Moon hanging over a calendar page marked with the number 1, casting silver light on the page
memory_hook.connection: सोम means the Moon — the silver Moon directly over the first day of the week locks in both the sound 'som' and the meaning 'Monday'

examples.hindi: सोमवार को मेरी मीटिंग है।
examples.romanisation: somvār ko merī mīṭiṅg hai.
examples.english: I have a meeting on Monday.
examples.literal: Monday on my meeting is

examples.hindi: हर सोमवार मैं जिम जाता हूँ।
examples.romanisation: har somvār maĩ jim jātā hū̃.
examples.english: Every Monday I go to the gym.
examples.literal: every Monday I gym go am

examples.hindi: सोमवार से शुक्रवार तक दफ़्तर खुला रहता है।
examples.romanisation: somvār se śukravār tak daftar khulā rahtā hai.
examples.english: The office is open from Monday to Friday.
examples.literal: Monday from Friday until office open remains is

examples.hindi: अगला सोमवार छुट्टी का दिन है।
examples.romanisation: aglā somvār chuṭṭī kā din hai.
examples.english: Next Monday is a holiday.
examples.literal: next Monday holiday's day is

true_relatives.meaning: Tiw's day (Norse god of war, parallel to Mars)
true_relatives.note: Both Tuesday and मंगलवार are named after a war deity — English after the Norse Tiw, Hindi after the Roman/Sanskrit Mars (Maṅgala)

useful_coincidences.note: Sounds like the start of मंगल — useful as a quick sonic hook even though the meanings are unrelated

sound_alikes.note: The opening 'maṅg' is very close to 'mang' in mango — picture a mango on a Tuesday market stall
мангал (mangal) means a barbecue grill in Russian (borrowed from Turkic) — same sound, easy to remember as the 'fiery' Mars-day

root.meaning: auspicious; the planet Mars; the god of war

root_breakdown.logic: मंगल means both 'auspicious' and 'Mars (the planet/god of war)'. The day was named after Mars, exactly as Latin dies Martis gave French Mardi and English Tuesday (via the parallel Norse war god Tiw).

etymology_story: The Sanskrit word मंगल carried a double meaning: 'auspicious, fortunate' and the name of the red planet Mars, the god of war.
In the Sanskrit seven-day week, each day was assigned to a celestial body — Tuesday was given to Mars (Maṅgala).
The form maṅgalavāsara shortened to मंगलवार in Hindi, preserving the planetary name in the weekday to this day.

pronunciation_guide.stress: roughly even across all three syllables; the nasal 'ṅ' is light

tips.tip: the first two syllables 'maṅgal' open exactly like 'mango' — same nasal vowel and 'g'
'maṅgal' is identical to the Russian word мангал (barbecue grill)

memory_hook.primary: A bright red planet (Mars) hanging in the night sky, with a glowing orange mango-shaped outline around it
memory_hook.connection: The red Mars planet encodes मंगल (the planet Mars), and the mango shape reinforces the opening sound 'maṅg', together anchoring मंगलवार as Tuesday

examples.hindi: मंगलवार को बाज़ार बंद रहता है।
examples.romanisation: maṅgalvār ko bāzār band rahtā hai.
examples.english: The market is closed on Tuesday.
examples.literal: Tuesday on market closed remains is

examples.hindi: हर मंगलवार को मंदिर में भीड़ होती है।
examples.romanisation: har maṅgalvār ko mandir mẽ bhīṛ hotī hai.
examples.english: Every Tuesday there is a crowd at the temple.
examples.literal: every Tuesday on temple in crowd happens is

examples.hindi: मंगलवार को मेरा इंटरव्यू है।
examples.romanisation: maṅgalvār ko merā iṇṭarvyu hai.
examples.english: My interview is on Tuesday.
examples.literal: Tuesday on my interview is

examples.hindi: सोमवार के बाद मंगलवार आता है।
examples.romanisation: somvār ke bād maṅgalvār ātā hai.
examples.english: Tuesday comes after Monday.
examples.literal: Monday after Tuesday comes is

true_relatives.meaning: day of Mercury / day of the wise one
true_relatives.note: Direct source — बुध (Mercury; wise one) + वासर (day)
Both Wednesday and बुधवार are named after the planet Mercury / the messenger-wise deity — English via Woden, Hindi via the Sanskrit Budha

useful_coincidences.note: बुध and बुद्ध share the same Sanskrit root बुध् meaning 'to know/awaken' — both evoke wisdom and intelligence

sound_alikes.note: 'Budh' sounds exactly like the first syllable of 'Buddha' — both come from the same Sanskrit root meaning wisdom

root.meaning: to know, to awaken, to be wise

root_breakdown.logic: The root बुध् means 'to know'. Budha (not the same as Buddha, though related) was the Sanskrit name for the planet Mercury, associated with intelligence and communication — exactly like Mercury in Roman mythology.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit verbal root बुध् means 'to know, to be awake' — it gave rise to both बुध (Mercury, the wise planet) and बुद्ध (the Awakened One).
In the Sanskrit astronomical week, Wednesday was assigned to Mercury (Budha), the swift and intelligent planet.
The form budhavāsara contracted to बुधवार in Hindi, keeping Mercury's name alive in every Wednesday.

pronunciation_guide.stress: roughly even; the 'dh' is an aspirated d — breathy, not like English 'th'

tips.tip: the first syllable 'budh' sounds like 'Buddh' in 'Buddha' — same aspirated dh sound

memory_hook.primary: A statue of the Buddha sitting cross-legged on a calendar page showing the middle of the week, surrounded by the symbol of Mercury (the winged staff)
memory_hook.connection: The Buddha image encodes 'budh' (same Sanskrit root), and the Mercury symbol confirms this is the planet Mercury's day — Wednesday

examples.hindi: बुधवार को हमारी क्लास नहीं होती।
examples.romanisation: budhvār ko hamārī klās nahī̃ hotī.
examples.english: We don't have class on Wednesday.
examples.literal: Wednesday on our class not happens

examples.hindi: मंगलवार और बुधवार को दुकान बंद है।
examples.romanisation: maṅgalvār aur budhvār ko dukān band hai.
examples.english: The shop is closed on Tuesday and Wednesday.
examples.literal: Tuesday and Wednesday on shop closed is

examples.hindi: बुधवार को मैं दिल्ली जाऊँगा।
examples.romanisation: budhvār ko maĩ dillī jāū̃gā.
examples.english: I will go to Delhi on Wednesday.
examples.literal: Wednesday on I Delhi will-go

examples.hindi: इस बुधवार का मौसम बहुत अच्छा था।
examples.romanisation: is budhvār kā mausam bahut acchā thā.
examples.english: The weather this Wednesday was very nice.
examples.literal: this Wednesday's weather very good was

true_relatives.meaning: day of Jupiter / day of the teacher
Thor's day (Norse thunder god, parallel to Jupiter)
true_relatives.note: Both Thursday and गुरुवार are named after the king of the gods / thunder deity — English via Thor, Hindi via Jupiter's Sanskrit name Guru/Bṛhaspati
The English word 'guru' is borrowed directly from Sanskrit गुरु — Thursday is literally 'the guru's day'

sound_alikes.note: The English word 'guru' (borrowed from Sanskrit) is the first part of गुरुवार — Thursday is literally the 'guru's day'

root.meaning: heavy, weighty; teacher; the planet Jupiter

root_breakdown.logic: गुरु means 'heavy' (the heaviest planet) and 'teacher / spiritual guide'. Jupiter, the largest planet, was seen as the great teacher among planets. Thursday is the guru's day.

etymology_story: The Sanskrit word गुरु originally meant 'heavy, weighty' — applied to Jupiter as the largest and most majestic planet.
गुरु also became the word for a teacher or spiritual guide, someone of great weight and wisdom.
Thursday was assigned to Jupiter in the Sanskrit week, giving गुरुवार — a day that carries both the planet and the teacher's name.

pronunciation_guide.stress: light and even across all syllables

tips.tip: the first two syllables 'guru' are pronounced exactly like the English word 'guru'

memory_hook.primary: A wise, bearded teacher (guru) sitting beneath a giant planet Jupiter — the planet glowing gold above his head on a calendar showing Thursday
memory_hook.connection: The guru figure encodes गुरु (teacher / Jupiter), and the planet above confirms this is Jupiter's day — गुरुवार / Thursday

examples.hindi: गुरुवार को मेरे गुरुजी का जन्मदिन है।
examples.romanisation: guruvār ko mere gurujī kā janmadin hai.
examples.english: My teacher's birthday is on Thursday.
examples.literal: Thursday on my teacher's birthday is

examples.hindi: हर गुरुवार को हम योग करते हैं।
examples.romanisation: har guruvār ko ham yog karte haĩ.
examples.english: We do yoga every Thursday.
examples.literal: every Thursday on we yoga do are

examples.hindi: बुधवार के बाद गुरुवार आता है।
examples.romanisation: budhvār ke bād guruvār ātā hai.
examples.english: Thursday comes after Wednesday.
examples.literal: Wednesday after Thursday comes is

examples.hindi: गुरुवार को दफ़्तर जल्दी बंद होता है।
examples.romanisation: guruvār ko daftar jaldī band hotā hai.
examples.english: The office closes early on Thursday.
examples.literal: Thursday on office early closed happens

true_relatives.meaning: day of Venus / day of brightness
true_relatives.note: Direct source — शुक्र (Venus; bright, brilliant) + वासर (day)

true_relatives.meaning: Frigg's day (Norse goddess of love, parallel to Venus)
true_relatives.note: Both Friday and शुक्रवार are named after the love/beauty goddess or planet Venus — English via Frigg, Hindi via the Sanskrit Venus (Śukra)

useful_coincidences.note: शुक्र and 'sugar' share a distant Indo-European connection via the Sanskrit śarkarā (sugar/gravel) — different root but similar sound helps as a memory hook

sound_alikes.note: 'śukr' sounds close to 'sugar' — both start with the same 'sh/s' + 'u' pattern, useful as a sonic bridge to remember शुक्रवार

root.meaning: bright, brilliant, shining; the planet Venus; the preceptor of the demons (Asuras)

root_breakdown.logic: शुक्र means 'bright, shining' — Venus is the brightest planet in the sky. Friday was assigned to Venus, just as Latin dies Veneris (Venus's day) became French Vendredi and English Friday (via the parallel Norse goddess Frigg).

etymology_story: The Sanskrit adjective शुक्र means 'bright, shining, pure' — it was the name given to Venus, the most brilliant planet visible to the naked eye.
In Sanskrit astronomical tradition, each day of the week was assigned a ruling planet; Friday was ruled by Venus (Śukra).
The form śukravāsara contracted to शुक्रवार in Hindi, so the very word for Friday still carries the brightness of Venus inside it.

pronunciation_guide.stress: roughly even; the 'ś' is a soft 'sh' sound

tips.tip: the first syllable 'śuk' begins like 'sugar' — same 'sh-u' opening

memory_hook.primary: A brilliant white planet Venus glowing in a twilight sky, with rays of light shaped like a shining star, over a calendar page showing the last workday of the week
memory_hook.connection: The shining Venus encodes शुक्र (bright / Venus), and its position at the week's end anchors it to Friday — शुक्रवार

examples.hindi: शुक्रवार को हम जल्दी घर जाते हैं।
examples.romanisation: śukravār ko ham jaldī ghar jāte haĩ.
examples.english: On Friday we go home early.
examples.literal: Friday on we early home go are

examples.hindi: गुरुवार के बाद शुक्रवार आता है।
examples.romanisation: guruvār ke bād śukravār ātā hai.
examples.english: Friday comes after Thursday.
examples.literal: Thursday after Friday comes is

examples.hindi: शुक्रवार की शाम बाज़ार में बहुत भीड़ होती है।
examples.romanisation: śukravār kī śām bāzār mẽ bahut bhīṛ hotī hai.
examples.english: Friday evening the market is very crowded.
examples.literal: Friday's evening market in very crowd happens is

examples.hindi: क्या तुम शुक्रवार को फ़्री हो?
examples.romanisation: kyā tum śukravār ko frī ho?
examples.english: Are you free on Friday?
examples.literal: what you Friday on free are
